title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problem_statement: Return the maximum depth (height) of a binary tree, defined as the number of nodes along the longest root-to-leaf path.
constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 10^4]
intuition: Compute the height of left and right subtrees and take the larger one, adding one for the current node.

approaches.name: Recursive DFS height
approaches.description: Return 1 plus the maximum of the depths of the left and right subtrees.
approaches.why_not: Breadth-first search also works but requires storing a queue of nodes at each level.
interview_commentary: The height is one plus the maximum height of the children.
Recursive DFS is the simplest way to compute tree height.

approaches.name: Iterative BFS height
approaches.description: Use a queue to traverse level by level and count the number of levels processed.
approaches.why_not: Recursive DFS has smaller auxiliary space in balanced trees and is simpler.
interview_commentary: Each BFS layer corresponds to one more depth level.
Count the number of levels until the queue is empty.
This is useful when explicit level information is helpful.

interview_qa.q: What is the recurrence for tree height?

interview_qa.a: Each BFS level corresponds to one depth increment.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between tree height and node count?
interview_qa.a: Height counts nodes along the longest path, not total nodes.

interview_qa.q: What is the worst-case stack depth?
interview_qa.a: O(h), where h is the tree height.
